excerpts.text: I have a great desire to go to China. I believe God has called me there, and I must obey, regardless of what friends or family may say.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to H.A. Tupper, Foreign Mission Board

excerpts.text: It is odd that a million Baptists of the South can furnish only three men for all China. I wonder how this looks in heaven.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the Foreign Mission Board

excerpts.text: I pray that no missionary will ever be as lonely as I have been.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to H.A. Tupper

excerpts.text: What women want who come to China is free opportunity to do the largest possible work. What women have a right to demand is perfect equality.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the Foreign Mission Journal

excerpts.text: I would I had a thousand lives that I might give them to the women of China.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the Foreign Mission Board

excerpts.text: Simple justice demands that women should have equal rights with men in mission meetings and in the conduct of their decrees.
excerpts.source_title: Letter published in the Foreign Mission Journal

excerpts.text: I go to Pingtu tomorrow. The people there are hungry for the gospel. They come in such numbers that I cannot teach them all.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to Annie Armstrong

excerpts.text: The needs of these people press upon me beyond what I can bear. Is there not one woman in all Virginia who will come and help me?
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the Foreign Mission Board

excerpts.text: I went out to live among the people in the country. It is the only way to reach them. You cannot win souls from a mission compound.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to H.A. Tupper

excerpts.text: These Chinese women have never heard of Jesus. Not once. In their whole lives, not a single word of the gospel. How can we sit in comfort at home?
excerpts.source_title: Appeal published in the Foreign Mission Journal

excerpts.text: I do not believe in a missionary life of self-indulgence. I eat what the Chinese eat. I dress as nearly as possible as they dress. I live among them.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the Foreign Mission Board

excerpts.text: The country work brings me great joy. I sometimes travel thirty or forty miles to preach in a village where no foreigner has ever been seen.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to Annie Armstrong

excerpts.text: The Christmas offering should be a special offering from women, by women, for women missionaries. Let the women of our churches give sacrificially.
excerpts.source_title: Appeal to the Woman's Missionary Union

excerpts.text: The famine is terrible. My neighbors are dying. I cannot eat while they starve. I have given away everything I have.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the Foreign Mission Board during famine

excerpts.text: The Chinese do not want our Western civilization. They want the gospel, pure and simple.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the Foreign Mission Board

excerpts.text: I shall remain at my post. Whether I live or die is in God's hands. But I will not desert the people who have come to depend on me.
excerpts.source_title: Letter during Boxer period

excerpts.text: My health is failing, but my heart is strong. Every day I see new faces turning to Christ, and that is all the medicine I need.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the Foreign Mission Board

excerpts.text: I am now more than sixty years old. I have spent nearly forty years in China. I cannot imagine having spent them anywhere else.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to family in Virginia

excerpts.text: I beg, I implore, I entreat the women of our Baptist churches to wake up to the duty of supporting woman's work in China.
excerpts.source_title: Appeal to WMU Convention

excerpts.text: There can be no doubt that the Chinese are as capable of receiving the gospel as any people on earth. The obstacle is not in them — it is in our neglect.
excerpts.source_title: Foreign Mission Journal article

excerpts.text: I came to Tengchow expecting to teach in the school. But God had other plans. He sent me to the villages, and that is where the real harvest is.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to H.A. Tupper

excerpts.text: Hardship and loneliness are part of the missionary's lot. But they are nothing compared to the joy of seeing a Chinese soul come to the Savior.
excerpts.source_title: Letter to the Foreign Mission Board

excerpts.text: I made cookies to attract the village children. Through the children, I reached the mothers. Through the mothers, I reached the families.
excerpts.source_title: Account of evangelistic methods in Pingtu

excerpts.text: God forbid that we should be content to live in luxury while our sisters in China perish without hope.
excerpts.source_title: Address to Woman's Missionary Union

excerpts.text: I am weighing only fifty pounds. They say I must leave China. But how can I leave when there is still so much work to be done?
excerpts.source_title: Account of her final illness, recorded by colleagues
